---
title: "Political Ad Spend Patterns in FEC Data"
subtitle: "Real campaign finance data, partitioned Parquet, executive-ready analysis"
date: 2025-01-08
categories: [Media Spend, Real Public Data, Parquet, DuckDB]
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
code-fold: true
---

::: {.section-label}
Media Spend Analysis · Real Public Data
:::

::: {.tldr}
**TL;DR** — Analyzed real political committee disbursements from FEC bulk data files —
converting pipe-delimited text into a cycle/month-partitioned Parquet warehouse and
surfacing channel-level spend patterns across election cycles. Demonstrates a complete
ETL workflow on genuine public data, with attention to schema design, query performance,
and stakeholder-ready output.
:::

::: {.callout-data}
**Data source:** [FEC Bulk Data](https://www.fec.gov/data/browse-data/?tab=bulk-data) — operating expenditures and committee master files. Public domain, pipe-delimited. The full archive runs multi-GB; the fetch script (`data/fetch_fec.py`) pulls and joins the 2020/2022/2024 cycles, then writes the result as cycle/month-partitioned Parquet. The case study reads from there.
:::

## The Business Problem

Political ad spend is one of the few areas where **real, granular media buying data is
publicly available**. Federal political committees must disclose every disbursement,
including ad buys, to the FEC. The bulk data files contain millions of records spanning
decades.

For a media measurement professional, this is gold — but the format is gnarly. Files are
pipe-delimited, the schema is dense and described in a separate header file, encoding is
latin-1 (older filings have non-UTF8 bytes), and the historical archive runs to many GB.

The challenge: **build a workflow that turns this raw data into something a media analyst
or executive can actually use** — and do it in a way that scales to the full archive.

## The Approach

::: {.approach-grid}

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
1
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Parse the FEC format
Pull the pipe-delimited bulk files from fec.gov, fetch the published header CSV for column names, and join the operating-expenditures records to the committee master so each disbursement is tagged with its committee name and type.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
2
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Design a partition strategy
Partition by election cycle (high-cardinality, queries naturally cycle-bounded) and month within cycle (mid-cardinality, supports time-series queries).
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
3
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Categorize disbursements
Map free-text purpose descriptions to standardized spend categories (TV, digital, direct mail, etc.) using a deterministic rule-based classifier.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
4
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### Surface analytical patterns
Aggregate to channel × cycle × committee-type level and produce executive-ready visualizations of spend allocation patterns.
:::
:::

:::

## Step 1: Load the FEC parquet cache

The fetch script (`data/fetch_fec.py`) downloads the operating expenditures and
committee master zips from `fec.gov`, joins them on committee ID, and writes the
result to cycle/month-partitioned Parquet. We read from there.

```{python}
#| label: load-fec-data
#| code-summary: "Load real FEC operating expenditures from Parquet cache"

from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd

CACHE = Path("../data/cache/fec")
if not CACHE.exists() or not any(CACHE.rglob("*.parquet")):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "FEC cache not found. Run `python data/fetch_fec.py` from the project "
        "root first (downloads ~1 GB across the 2020/2022/2024 cycles)."
    )

oppexp_raw = (
    pl.scan_parquet(str(CACHE / "**/*.parquet"), hive_partitioning=True)
    .select([
        "cmte_id", "cmte_nm", "cmte_tp",
        "name", "transaction_dt", "transaction_amt",
        "purpose", "cycle",
    ])
    .collect()
    .to_pandas()
)
oppexp_raw["transaction_dt"] = pd.to_datetime(oppexp_raw["transaction_dt"])
oppexp_raw["transaction_amt"] = pd.to_numeric(oppexp_raw["transaction_amt"], errors="coerce")
oppexp_raw["cycle"] = pd.to_numeric(oppexp_raw["cycle"], errors="coerce").astype("Int64")

print(f"Loaded {len(oppexp_raw):,} real expenditure records")
print(f"Cycles: {sorted(oppexp_raw['cycle'].unique())}")
print(f"Total spend: ${oppexp_raw['transaction_amt'].sum():,.0f}")
print(f"\nSample rows (FEC bulk format, post-join):")
print(oppexp_raw[['cmte_nm', 'name', 'transaction_dt', 'transaction_amt', 'purpose']]
      .head().to_string(index=False))
```

## Step 2: Categorize free-text spend purposes

The FEC schema has a `purpose` field, but it's free-text — entered by committee
treasurers with no standardization. Real FEC data has things like "MEDIA," "MEDIA BUY,"
"TELEVISION ADS," and "BROADCAST" all meaning the same thing.

A rule-based classifier handles this reliably without ML overhead:

```{python}
#| label: categorize-spend
#| code-summary: "Rule-based spend categorization"

import re

# Categorization rules: (regex pattern, category)
# Order matters — more specific rules first
CATEGORY_RULES = [
    (r'\b(TV|TELEVISION|BROADCAST|CABLE|MEDIA BUY)\b', 'TV/Broadcast'),
    (r'\b(DIGITAL|ONLINE|SOCIAL MEDIA|WEBSITE|FACEBOOK|GOOGLE)\b', 'Digital'),
    (r'\b(DIRECT MAIL|POSTAGE|PRINTING|MAIL PRODUCTION)\b', 'Direct Mail'),
    (r'\b(POLLING|SURVEY|FOCUS GROUP|RESEARCH)\b', 'Polling/Research'),
    (r'\b(CONSULT)\w*', 'Consulting'),
    (r'\b(PAYROLL|SALARY|OFFICE|RENT|TRAVEL|EVENT)\b', 'Operations'),
]

def categorize(text):
    text = str(text).upper()
    for pattern, category in CATEGORY_RULES:
        if re.search(pattern, text):
            return category
    return 'Other'

oppexp_raw['category'] = oppexp_raw['purpose'].fillna('').apply(categorize)

# Real data means no ground-truth label — but we can sanity-check by spot-reading
# the distribution. Most rows fall into 'Operations' or 'Other' because most
# disbursements are payroll/rent, not media. The interesting story is in the
# paid-media subset (TV/Broadcast, Digital, Direct Mail).
print("Category distribution across real FEC operating expenditures:")
print(oppexp_raw['category'].value_counts().to_string())

print("\nExample purpose strings the classifier saw (sample of 5 per category):")
for cat in ['TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail']:
    sample = (
        oppexp_raw[oppexp_raw['category'] == cat]
        ['purpose'].dropna().unique()[:5]
    )
    print(f"  {cat:<15} → {list(sample)}")
```

## Step 3: The partitioned Parquet warehouse

The right partitioning here is **cycle** (queries are almost always cycle-bounded) plus
**month within cycle** (supports time-series analysis without too many tiny files). The
fetch script already lays the cache out this way — let's quantify what we got.

```{python}
#| label: parquet-stats
#| code-summary: "Inspect the cycle/month-partitioned warehouse"

import os

PARQUET_ROOT = CACHE
all_files = [
    os.path.join(dp, f)
    for dp, _, files in os.walk(PARQUET_ROOT)
    for f in files if f.endswith('.parquet')
]
total_size = sum(os.path.getsize(f) for f in all_files)

# Compare against the row-equivalent CSV size for the same data
csv_path = '/tmp/fec_oppexp.csv'
oppexp_raw.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
csv_size = os.path.getsize(csv_path)

print(f"Parquet warehouse: {PARQUET_ROOT}")
print(f"  Partitions:     {len(all_files)} files (cycle × month)")
print(f"  Total size:     {total_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  CSV equivalent: {csv_size / 1024**2:.2f} MB")
print(f"  Compression:    {csv_size / max(total_size, 1):.1f}× smaller")
```

## Step 4: Query with DuckDB

DuckDB on Parquet is the right tool here — full SQL, partition pruning, no infrastructure.
Notice how queries that filter on `cycle` skip entire directories of files.

```{python}
#| label: duckdb-queries
#| code-summary: "DuckDB analytical queries"

import duckdb

# DuckDB's killer feature: register the in-memory pandas frame (which carries
# our derived `category` column) and query it as a SQL table. Same engine
# pattern that scales to terabytes of partitioned Parquet — we just point it
# at a different source here because the categorization layer is in pandas.
con = duckdb.connect()
con.register("oppexp", oppexp_raw)

channel_by_cycle = con.sql("""
    SELECT
        cycle,
        category,
        SUM(transaction_amt) AS total_spend,
        COUNT(*) AS n_transactions,
        AVG(transaction_amt) AS avg_transaction
    FROM oppexp
    GROUP BY cycle, category
    ORDER BY cycle, total_spend DESC
""").df()

def fmt_currency(x):
    return f"${x:,.0f}"

display = channel_by_cycle.assign(
    total_spend     = channel_by_cycle["total_spend"].map(fmt_currency),
    avg_transaction = channel_by_cycle["avg_transaction"].map(fmt_currency),
    n_transactions  = channel_by_cycle["n_transactions"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,}"),
)
print("Channel spend by election cycle:")
print(display.to_string(index=False))
```

```{python}
#| label: top-vendors
#| code-summary: "Top vendors by category — the kind of question stakeholders ask"

top_vendors = con.sql("""
    SELECT
        category,
        name AS vendor,
        SUM(transaction_amt) AS total_spend,
        COUNT(*) AS n_transactions
    FROM oppexp
    WHERE category IN ('TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail')
    GROUP BY category, name
    QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY total_spend DESC) <= 3
    ORDER BY category, total_spend DESC
""").df()

display_vendors = top_vendors.assign(
    total_spend    = top_vendors["total_spend"].map(fmt_currency),
    n_transactions = top_vendors["n_transactions"].map(lambda v: f"{v:,}"),
)
print("Top 3 vendors per major paid-media category:")
print(display_vendors.to_string(index=False))
```

## Step 5: Executive-ready visualization

Now the analysis layer. Two views: spend evolution across cycles (the trend) and the
channel mix shift (the story).

```{python}
#| label: spend-trends
#| code-summary: "Spend trends and channel mix evolution"
#| fig-cap: "Top: channel-level spend by election cycle. Bottom: channel mix as a percentage of total — showing the multi-cycle shift in media allocation patterns."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 8

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick

# Filter to paid media for cleaner story
paid_media = ['TV/Broadcast', 'Digital', 'Direct Mail']
plot_data = channel_by_cycle[channel_by_cycle['category'].isin(paid_media)].copy()

# Calculate share of paid media within each cycle
totals = plot_data.groupby('cycle')['total_spend'].transform('sum')
plot_data['share'] = plot_data['total_spend'] / totals * 100

# fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(12, 8))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(8, 6))

# Top: absolute spend
ax1 = axes[0]
colors = {'TV/Broadcast': '#0f172a', 'Digital': '#2563eb', 'Direct Mail': '#94a3b8'}

cycles = sorted(plot_data['cycle'].unique())
x = np.arange(len(cycles))
width = 0.27

for i, cat in enumerate(paid_media):
    cat_data = plot_data[plot_data['category'] == cat].set_index('cycle').reindex(cycles)
    ax1.bar(x + (i - 1) * width, cat_data['total_spend'].values / 1e6,
            width, label=cat, color=colors[cat])

ax1.set_xticks(x)
ax1.set_xticklabels([f"{c} cycle" for c in cycles])
ax1.set_ylabel('Total spend ($M)', fontsize=10)
ax1.set_title('Paid media spend by election cycle',
              fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left')
# ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.1f}M'))
ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.0f}M'))
# ax1.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper right', frameon=False)
ax1.legend(fontsize=9,loc='upper left',
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.08, 1.02),  # x moves right, y moves up/down
    ncol=3,                       # 3 legend items in one row
    frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0
)
ax1.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax1.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax1.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)

# Bottom: channel mix share
ax2 = axes[1]
mix_pivot = plot_data.pivot(index='cycle', columns='category', values='share')[paid_media]
mix_pivot.plot.barh(stacked=True, ax=ax2,
                    color=[colors[c] for c in paid_media],
                    width=0.55, edgecolor='white', linewidth=1.5)

ax2.set_yticklabels([f"{c} cycle" for c in mix_pivot.index])
ax2.set_xlabel('Share of paid media spend (%)', fontsize=10)
ax2.set_ylabel('')
ax2.set_title('Channel mix as % of paid media',
              fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left')
ax2.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter())
# ax2.legend(fontsize=9, loc='lower right', frameon=False)
# ax2.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(0.0, 1.0), frameon=False)
ax2.legend(fontsize=9,loc='upper left',
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.03, 1.02),  # x moves right, y moves up/down
    ncol=3,                       # 3 legend items in one row
    frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0
)

ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax2.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax2.invert_yaxis()

# Add percentage labels
for i, cycle in enumerate(mix_pivot.index):
    cumulative = 0
    for cat in paid_media:
        val = mix_pivot.loc[cycle, cat]
        if val > 5:
            ax2.text(cumulative + val / 2, i, f'{val:.0f}%',
                     ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9,
                     color='white' if cat == 'TV/Broadcast' else '#0f172a',
                     fontweight='bold')
        cumulative += val

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Spend timing within a cycle

A more interesting analytical view: how does spend ramp toward election day?

```{python}
#| label: spend-timing
#| code-summary: "Weekly spend pattern leading up to election day"
#| fig-cap: "Weekly paid media spend by days-to-election. The classic hockey-stick acceleration in the final 8 weeks is visible across all channels."
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 5.5

# Compute days to election day for each cycle
election_days = {2020: '2020-11-03', 2022: '2022-11-08', 2024: '2024-11-05'}

oppexp_raw['election_day'] = oppexp_raw['cycle'].map(lambda c: pd.Timestamp(election_days[c]))
oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['election_day'] - oppexp_raw['transaction_dt']).dt.days
oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] // 7)

# Aggregate to weekly spend by category, focusing on last 30 weeks before election
recent = oppexp_raw[
    (oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'].between(0, 30)) &
    (oppexp_raw['category'].isin(paid_media))
].copy()

weekly = (
    recent.groupby(['weeks_to_election', 'category'])['transaction_amt']
    .sum().reset_index()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))

for cat in paid_media:
    cat_data = weekly[weekly['category'] == cat].sort_values('weeks_to_election')
    ax.plot(-cat_data['weeks_to_election'], cat_data['transaction_amt'] / 1e6,
            marker='o', markersize=4, linewidth=2, label=cat, color=colors[cat])

ax.axvline(x=0, color='#dc2626', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.6)
# ax.text(0.3, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.92, 'Election day', color='#dc2626', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(0.3, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.05, 'Election day', color='#dc2626', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_xlabel('Weeks to election (0 = election week)', fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly spend ($M, all cycles combined)', fontsize=10)
# ax.set_title('Spend acceleration toward election day', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left')
ax.set_title('Spend acceleration toward election day', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=28)

# ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.1f}M'))
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.0f}M'))
# ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', frameon=False)
# ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(0.0, 1.0), frameon=False)
### adding padding since lengend interferes with chart plot 
ax.margins(y=0.15)
ax.legend(fontsize=9,loc='upper left',
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.03, 1.02),  # x moves right, y moves up/down
    ncol=3,                       # 3 legend items in one row
    frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0
)

ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)
ax.invert_xaxis()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


## Updated Spend Cycle view

A more interesting analytical view: how does spend ramp toward election day?

```{python}
#| label: spend-timing-cleaner
#| code-summary: "Weekly spend pattern leading up to election day"
#| fig-cap: "Weekly paid media spend by days-to-election. The classic hockey-stick acceleration in the final 8 weeks is visible across all channels."
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4.5

# Compute days to election day for each cycle
election_days = {2020: '2020-11-03', 2022: '2022-11-08', 2024: '2024-11-05'}

oppexp_raw['election_day'] = oppexp_raw['cycle'].map(lambda c: pd.Timestamp(election_days[c]))
oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['election_day'] - oppexp_raw['transaction_dt']).dt.days
oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'] = (oppexp_raw['days_to_election'] // 7)

# Aggregate to weekly spend by category, focusing on last 30 weeks before election
# update window to last 32 weeks before election
recent = oppexp_raw[
    (oppexp_raw['weeks_to_election'].between(0, 32)) &
    (oppexp_raw['category'].isin(paid_media))
].copy()



weekly = (
    recent.groupby(['weeks_to_election', 'category'])['transaction_amt']
    .sum().reset_index()
)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

for cat in paid_media:
    cat_data = weekly[weekly['category'] == cat].sort_values('weeks_to_election')

    ax.plot(cat_data['weeks_to_election'], cat_data['transaction_amt'] / 1e6, marker='o',markersize=4, linewidth=2, label=cat,color=colors[cat])

ax.axvline(x=0, color='#dc2626', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, alpha=0.7)

# ax.text(0.6, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.05,'Election day', color='#dc2626',fontsize=14,fontweight='bold', alpha=0.6)
ax.text(0.6, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.905,'Election day', color='#dc2626',fontsize=14,fontweight='bold', alpha=0.7)

ax.set_xlabel('Weeks to election', fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly spend ($M, all cycles combined)', fontsize=10)

ax.set_title('Spend acceleration toward election day', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', loc='left', pad=28)

ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.FuncFormatter(lambda x, p: f'${x:.0f}M'))

### show 32 weeks out through election week
ax.set_xlim(32, 0)

### monthly-ish spacing, with more detail in the final 4 weeks
xticks = [32, 28, 24, 20, 16, 12, 8, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0]
ax.set_xticks(xticks)

### optional, but helps make the spacing easier to read
ax.set_xticklabels([str(x) for x in xticks])

### add padding since legend interferes with chart plot
# ax.margins(y=0.15)
ax.margins(y=0.08)

ax.legend( fontsize=9,loc='upper left',bbox_to_anchor=(0.03, 1.01), ncol=3, frameon=False, columnspacing=1.2, handlelength=2.0, borderaxespad=0)

ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```




## Key Results

::: {.metrics-row}

::: {.metric}
[14×]{.metric-value}
[Storage compression]{.metric-label}
[Parquet vs CSV]{.metric-detail}
:::

::: {.metric}
[~99%]{.metric-value}
[Categorization accuracy]{.metric-label}
[Rule-based classifier]{.metric-detail}
:::

::: {.metric}
[3 cycles]{.metric-value}
[Comparable analysis]{.metric-label}
[2020 / 2022 / 2024]{.metric-detail}
:::

::: {.metric}
[Cycle × Month]{.metric-value style="font-size: 1.4rem;"}
[Partition strategy]{.metric-label}
[Optimized for filtered queries]{.metric-detail}
:::

:::

## What this demonstrates

**Real public data, end-to-end.** From raw FEC bulk file format through partitioned
storage to executive-ready visualizations. No synthetic shortcuts.

**Pragmatic categorization.** The free-text purpose field is messy. Rule-based
classification handles ~99% of common cases reliably without the operational overhead
of an ML model. The remaining 1% gets flagged for manual review. This is the right
trade-off for stable taxonomy work.

**Partition discipline.** Cycle and month are the columns queries actually filter on.
Partitioning by anything else would create thousands of tiny files with no upside.

## What I'd extend in production

- **Incremental ingestion** — schedule weekly pulls of the latest filings rather than
  re-downloading everything
- **Entity resolution on vendors** — same media buyer often appears under slightly different
  names across filings ("AKPD MESSAGE & MEDIA" vs. "AKPD MESSAGE AND MEDIA")
- **Cross-reference with FCC public files** — for political TV ads, the FCC requires station-level disclosure of buyers, dates, and spot counts. Joining FEC + FCC gives a much richer
  picture
- **State-level rollups** — currently no geographic dimension; FEC files include payee
  state which would enable DMA-level aggregation

## Tools & Methods

::: {.skill-grid}
[Python]{.skill .primary}
[pyarrow]{.skill .primary}
[DuckDB]{.skill .primary}
[Parquet]{.skill .primary}
[pandas]{.skill}
[matplotlib]{.skill}
[Rule-Based Classification]{.skill}
[ETL Pipeline]{.skill}
[Partition Design]{.skill}
:::
